message.content:
Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-03_11-02-34_game-of-life-prose_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Activate test: "should keep alive a living cell with two live neighbors"
Current state: 3 tests passing, 6 todo
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-03_11-02-34_game-of-life-prose_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts

Test should verify: Use a configuration where a cell has exactly 2 live neighbors and survives. Example: A diagonal of 3 cells [[0,0],[1,1],[2,2]] - the middle cell [1,1] has 2 neighbors and survives. The corners only have 1 neighbor each so die. Expected: nextGeneration([[0,0],[1,1],[2,2]]) should include [1,1]. Use toEqual with expect.arrayContaining or sort the result.

Better example: An L shape or vertical 3-cell column [[0,0],[0,1],[0,2]] - middle cell [0,1] has 2 neighbors and survives. Top and bottom each have 1 neighbor and die. But also new cells [1,1] and [-1,1] would each have 3 neighbors and become alive (reproduction) - so this is actually the blinker. Use a simpler config that only tests survival.

Actually, the cleanest survival-only test: place cells at [[0,0],[1,0],[2,0]] (horizontal row of 3) - middle [1,0] survives with 2 neighbors. But [1,1] and [1,-1] would also come alive (3 neighbors). To avoid the reproduction interaction, use a diagonal: [[0,0],[1,1],[2,2]]. Middle [1,1] has 2 live neighbors ([0,0] and [2,2]), survives. Cells [0,0] and [2,2] each have only 1 neighbor, die. No dead cell has 3 live neighbors so no births. Expected result: [[1,1]].

Use this test: expect(nextGeneration([[0,0],[1,1],[2,2]])).toEqual([[1,1]]);

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Green after completion.

content.text: I'll activate the test for survival with two live neighbors. Let me first examine the current state of the test and implementation files.
Now I'll activate the test for survival with two live neighbors.
